segments.text: So, today we are going to do the repeat of lecture 1 with the Mimio device.
So let us start with introducing the system of linear equations.
What exactly is a system of linear equations?
What exactly do we mean by a linear equation?
So let us basically take an example of two equations, something like say 3x is
equal to 1, x plus 2y is equal to 3.
So this we call as a system of linear equations, system because we have more than one equation.
And the reason we call them as linear is the variables that are appearing in this equation
x and y both are being raised to the first power.
So the powers of x and y less than or equal to 1, for example there is no y in the
first equation.
So the power of y is 0 and in the second equation both x and y appear as with powers 1.
Okay, now given the system of equations, we will introduce multiple ways of interpreting.
So there are two main ways we will focus on, one of them would be the geometrical
way or we will like to call it as the geometrical interpretation.
The other thing is what we are more interested in is the so called algebraic interpretation.
So now when I say geometrical interpretation, why are we interested in that geometrical
interpretation?
Because ultimately what will happen is in lower dimensions like the second, two dimensions
or three dimensions, we will be able to understand things because we can view them.
And from there we will be able to get some algebraic interpretation out of it.
As we move into higher dimensions, we will see that no longer our geometric intuition
is helpful and that is when we have to rely solely on algebraic methods.
But the foundation from lower dimensions which we will be able to visualize will
be able to help us do the algebra also.
So let us come back to a simple question.
When I say x is equal to 1, what exactly does this represent?
So the question we are interested in is what is represented by this particular equation?
Now when we first pose this question x is equal to 1 depending on what level you
are at, you might have different interpretation and that is what we are more interested
in is to ask you exactly how do you interpret x is equal to 1?
Does it represent a point?
Does it represent a line?
What does it actually represent?
This is where we have to be now very careful.
For example, if we are in one dimensional situation, then x is equal to 1 will actually
represent a point which marks the position on the real axis of the point we call as 1.
If you are in two dimensions, the interpretation of the same equation will be slightly different.
In two dimensions, when I say x is equal to 1, then basically what it is, is actually
a line which passes through the point 1 0, it is a vertical line which passes through
the point 1 0.
So as you can see depending on the dimensionality of this space, we have a different interpretation
associated with the same equation.
So when somebody says x is equal to 1 always represents a line, that is not an entirely
true statement.
In fact, if I go into third dimension, then this x is equal to 1 will be representing
a plane.
So it is no longer a collection of lines if I may say so.
So x is equal to 1, the best we can say is that it is an equation which represents
a geometrical object and depending on the dimensionality of the space we live in.
So for example, in three dimension, this plane is what we call as being represented
by x is equal to 1 assuming that it is a horizontal plane, this is x axis, y axis
and z axis.
So from now on whenever somebody gives us an equation or a system of equations, we
have to be very careful and ask ourselves what is the ambient space in which we are
working and based on that space we will try to interpret the answer of the solution
